---
title: "Contribute a Dataset"
---

StatsData Hub is an open community resource — contributions of new
datasets are welcome and encouraged. Each dataset needs two things:
a data file and a metadata description. Here's how to add one.

## What makes a good dataset for this repository?

A good teaching dataset should:

- Illustrate one or more **specific statistical methods** clearly
- Have a meaningful **real-world context** students can engage with
- Be **openly licensed** (CC0, CC BY, or similar) so others can freely use it
- Be appropriate for **classroom use** (no sensitive personal data)
- Come with a **citable source** (published study, public repository, or well-known teaching dataset)

## Step-by-step contribution guide

### 1. Fork the repository

Go to [github.com/emilysomerset/statsdata-hub](https://github.com/emilysomerset/statsdata-hub)
and click **Fork** (top right). This creates your own copy of the project
to work in.

### 2. Add the data file

Place your cleaned dataset as a CSV file at:

data/clean/<dataset-id>/<dataset-id>.csv

For example:

data/clean/titanic-survival/titanic-survival.csv

If the data required cleaning or processing, add your processing script at:

scripts/process-<dataset-id>.R

### 3. Create a metadata YAML file

Copy the template below and save it as:

metadata/datasets/<dataset-id>.yaml

Fill in every field — the more complete the metadata, the more useful
the dataset is for instructors searching for specific teaching needs.

```yaml
id: your-dataset-id
title: "Your Dataset Title"
description: >
  A clear description of what the dataset contains, where it came from,
  and what it is commonly used to teach.

source:
  citation: "Author(s) (Year). Title. Journal/Source."
  url: "https://link-to-source.com"
  license: "CC0-1.0"

file: "data/clean/your-dataset-id/your-dataset-id.csv"

data_source_type: published_study  # or: classic_dataset, public_repository, simulated

methods: [logistic_regression]     # see full list below
concepts: [confounding]            # see full list below
variable_types: [binary, continuous]
course_level: [introductory]       # introductory, intermediate, advanced_undergraduate, graduate
discipline: [health]               # see full list below

characteristics:
  n_obs: 1000
  n_vars: 10
  missingness: false
  survey_weights: false
  longitudinal: false
  hierarchical: false
  cross_sectional: true

teaching:
  learning_objectives:
    - "Learning objective 1"
    - "Learning objective 2"
  classroom_activities:
    - "Suggested activity 1"
  common_misconceptions:
    - "Common misconception 1"

variables:
  - name: column_name
    type: continuous
    description: "What this variable measures"
```

### 4. Open a pull request

Once you've added the data file and YAML, open a pull request on GitHub.
Describe what the dataset is and why it would be useful for teaching.
A maintainer will review it and let you know if anything needs updating.

## Valid metadata values

**Statistical methods:**
`t_test`, `anova`, `linear_regression`, `logistic_regression`,
`poisson_regression`, `survival_analysis`, `pca`, `clustering`,
`survey_sampling`, `bayesian_methods`, `time_series`,
`multilevel_modeling`, `factor_analysis`, `mixed_effects`

**Teaching concepts:**
`confounding`, `simpsons_paradox`, `missing_data`, `outliers`,
`heteroskedasticity`, `multicollinearity`, `nonlinearity`,
`selection_bias`, `clustered_observations`, `measurement_error`,
`overdispersion`

**Disciplines:**
`health`, `biology`, `psychology`, `economics`, `sports`,
`climate`, `education`, `survey_research`, `finance`, `politics`

## Questions?

Open an issue on [GitHub](https://github.com/emilysomerset/statsdata-hub/issues)
and I'll be happy to help.
